title: Who Lacks Concern and Foresight?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR-derived reading copy gave the closing date as 21 October 2013. The source scan visibly prints 27 October 2013, which is used here and in the derived Tamil reading copy. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: On 25 October 2013, the Communist parties raised a special calling-attention motion in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly demanding uninterrupted electricity for industries and farmers. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa delivered a lengthy reply. No one should ask whether the minister concerned ought to answer such a motion, or whether there is no Electricity Minister. That minister has other important work, and so the Chief Minister has answered on the principle of collective Cabinet responsibility!

We have no quarrel with the Chief Minister giving a long explanation about the power shortage. But the person who came forward to explain it unnecessarily accused us, saying, “Because of the previous minority DMK government's indifference, lack of foresight and administrative incompetence, Tamil Nadu, which once shone with light, was plunged into darkness.” That is why I am obliged to respond.

In particular, the Chief Minister declared in her long speech that Tamil Nadu would become a power-surplus state by the end of this year. A few months after Jayalalithaa assumed office for the third time, the Budget placed before the Assembly on 4 August 2011 said that power cuts would gradually be relaxed as generation increased and would be eliminated throughout the state by August 2012.

On 28 March 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa told the Assembly: “We announced that the duration of power cuts would be reduced gradually from June. We are only in March now. Wait another three months.” In an interview given to *India Today* in February 2012, her Electricity Minister said Tamil Nadu would experience a power shortage only from March to May 2012 and that, if people endured those three months, there would be no power shortage thereafter. In an interview in the issue of *Junior Vikatan* dated 28 November 2012, the same minister said, “I am confident that power cuts will be set right by June 2013.” The Chief Minister now says Tamil Nadu will become a power-surplus state by the end of this year.

The Chief Minister has alleged that the DMK government failed to formulate new power projects in time to match the demand rising year after year. When the DMK left office in 2011, the generation projects under way in Tamil Nadu were: North Chennai Stage II, Mettur, Thoothukudi, Vallur, Bhavani Kattalai and Udangudi. These are listed on page 151 of the *Statistical Handbook of Tamil Nadu 2011*.

On 2 November 2012, Jayalalithaa listed in the Assembly several projects that would soon begin generation. The Mettur project of 600 MW had begun on 25 June 2008; Vallur Units I, II and III of 500 MW each on 13 August 2007; North Chennai Units I and II of 600 MW each on 18 February 2008; and the two Thoothukudi units totalling 1,000 MW on 28 January 2009. All seven projects were started under the DMK government.

In the statement she read yesterday, Jayalalithaa first said that the DMK government had not planned new generation projects in time. Yet she herself said, “In the case of the 600 MW Mettur Thermal Power Project, only 55 per cent of the work was completed during the five years of the previous minority DMK government.” Is that not her own admission that 55 per cent of the Mettur project was completed under the DMK government?

But she makes an accusation out of saying that only 55 per cent was completed in five years. Is even that true? Work on the Mettur project began only on 25 June 2008. The DMK government did not remain in office for five years after that, but only three. Is it fair for a Chief Minister to conceal even this fact and give the Tamil Nadu Assembly the false information that only 55 per cent of the work was completed in five years?

After the AIADMK came to office in 2011, its demand for grants placed before the Assembly said that the Mettur project would become operational in March 2012. Contrary to its own promise, the project did not become operational in 2012 and was delayed by another year. Which government caused that delay?

Jayalalithaa has spoken in the same way about the North Chennai thermal-power projects. She admits that they were begun under the DMK government, but claims electricity is available now only because her government accelerated all these projects. Having told the Assembly in 2011 that North Chennai would begin generation in January 2012, if generation is beginning only now, who caused the delay? Which government? If Jayalalithaa claims credit for the electricity generated by these projects, will she tell us how much money her government allocated to them?

Had the DMK government not begun these projects, would even this electricity be available now? It is only because we began them that she has been able to complete them. Jayalalithaa may ask whether power would have become available had her government not continued the projects. But if it had failed to continue them, would opposition parties such as the DMK have remained silent? However much Jayalalithaa tries to conceal it, the people of Tamil Nadu know very well that the additional electricity now available comes from projects implemented under the DMK government.

Jayalalithaa told the Assembly yesterday: “Work on the three 500 MW units at Vallur in North Chennai, established during my previous term, moved at a tortoise's pace under the previous minority DMK government. After we assumed office, we accelerated the work, and as a result two units began commercial generation on 29 November 2012 and 25 August 2013 respectively.”

The Vallur project did not begin under Jayalalithaa's rule. It was inaugurated on 13 August 2007, during the DMK government, by then Union Power Minister Thiru Shinde. When the project began, generation was tentatively scheduled to start in the first unit in December 2012 and in the second in March 2013. Yet Jayalalithaa says the project moved at a tortoise's pace under the DMK. She gave completion percentages for all the other projects. Why did she alone omit the percentage completed at Vallur? She also states that generation began there as early as 29 November 2012. Anyone can therefore understand that the efforts made during the DMK government were responsible for it.

Jayalalithaa said yesterday, “As the above new thermal projects have been completed and begun generation, we are now receiving an additional 1,700 MW.” Every bit of that 1,700 MW comes from projects begun under the DMK government. Can anyone deny it? Jayalalithaa may try to hide the DMK government's concern and foresight in beginning generation projects, but the facts themselves reveal the truth.

She has arranged to purchase electricity from outside the state from June this year and says it is now being received. Her government is also trying to obtain additional electricity on a long-term basis. But are adequate transmission corridors available to bring electricity purchased from other states? Jayalalithaa herself says those corridors are only now being built and are expected to become operational from 2014. In the 2012–13 Budget, this government itself said there was no way to transmit electricity from other states. How, then, did it proceed to buy so much electricity from them? Whatever the explanation, it cannot relieve the present shortage.

Jayalalithaa said yesterday that Tamil Nadu was a power-surplus state during the AIADMK government from 2001 to 2006. Was that not because of the generation projects created by the DMK government before 2001? She now blames the preceding DMK government for failing to implement generation projects and causing the present shortage. But when she says Tamil Nadu was once power-surplus, should not the earlier DMK government receive the credit? She says we lacked the administrative ability to implement power projects quickly. For nearly three years now, because the AIADMK government itself lacks that ability, Tamil Nadu has remained power-starved and has not implemented a single new project.

Jayalalithaa says the DMK government failed to arrange long-term power purchases as early as 2008. The administratively incompetent Jayalalithaa assumed office after the 2011 general election. Had we known in 2008 that this shortage would arise, would we not have entered into long-term purchases then? Whatever the subject, the phrase Jayalalithaa repeats is “administrative incompetence.”

The person who served as Finance Secretary under the previous DMK government and advised us in every meeting is now Chairman of the Electricity Board. The person who was Finance Secretary then continues as Finance Secretary now. The government functioned then, as it does now, after receiving the advice of such senior officials. Those officials know well that the DMK government made important decisions after taking their counsel, not in Jayalalithaa's present style of acting first and overturning things at will.

I have cited evidence and explained the power-generation projects that Jayalalithaa herself admits were begun under the DMK government. I now ask: the AIADMK has been in office for two and a half years since 2011. How many power-generation projects has it actually started?

In the AIADMK government's 2011–12 policy note, you said work would begin in 2012 on North Chennai Stages III and IV, Udangudi, the Ennore Link and the Kundah pumped-storage hydroelectric projects, with an estimated cost of Rs 28,800 crore. Has work begun on even one of them?

And on page 12 of the same policy note, you announced that projects producing 8,000 MW would be launched at an estimated cost of Rs 22,800 crore: the Udangudi expansion, Uppur Thermal Power Station, replacement of the Ennore Thermal Power Station, and Thoothukudi Thermal Power Station Stage IV. Has even one of them materialised in these two and a half years? Are you prepared to answer?

Speaking of these projects yesterday, the Chief Minister merely said that tenders were under examination and orders to establish the projects would soon be issued so that work could begin. She did not say that a single project had begun. Why? The projects were announced in 2011. At the end of 2013, tenders are still under examination. Is this how the government functions?

Jayalalithaa said yesterday that Delta farmers have continuously received twelve hours of three-phase electricity every day since 15 June 2013. We will be happy if the Delta farmers and the Communist parties agree with that claim.

Jayalalithaa next said that the DMK government had continually deceived the people and imposed power cuts without even announcing them, whereas her government now supplies electricity without any power cuts at all. The people of Tamil Nadu know the shortage from their own experience. Only a few months ago, Tamil Nadu was severely affected by it, and small industries in districts such as Coimbatore and Tiruppur suffered enormously. Jayalalithaa imagines she can hide all this as though concealing an entire pumpkin in a plate of rice.

Jayalalithaa says, “Additional installed capacity was created during my government from 2001 to 2006.” Do you know the total amount spent on power generation during those years? A mere Rs 700 crore. Do you know how much the DMK government spent on generation from 2006 to 2011? Rs 11,700 crore.

Jayalalithaa said yesterday: “In the last Assembly session, I announced that the 2,000 MW Sillahalla pumped-storage hydroelectric project would be created in the Nilgiris district at an estimated cost of Rs 7,000 crore. Survey work began on 22 August 2013 and is under way.” Do you know when she first announced it? It was in the statement she read under Rule 110 on 25 April 2013. Six months later, the Chief Minister tells the Assembly only that survey work has begun. You said the estimate was Rs 7,000 crore. How much money has been allocated so far? Whom are these project announcements meant to deceive?

The Chief Minister said yesterday that she had announced another project estimated at Rs 5,000 crore, including Rs 3,572 crore in Japanese financial assistance. Let me tell you when she announced that project too. It appeared in the same Rule 110 statement Jayalalithaa read on 25 April 2013. Six months after the announcement, she told the Assembly yesterday that tenders had been invited. Must I explain how swiftly the AIADMK government works?

Next, Jayalalithaa said she had released the “Tamil Nadu Solar Energy Policy 2012” to increase generation from non-conventional sources and that it would produce 3,000 MW of solar power within three years. But here too, tenders have been called and no decision has been taken.

The Chief Minister spoke at such length in the Assembly about electricity generation, yet did not open her mouth about wind power. The 2011 policy note said private producers planned to generate 10,000 MW of wind power. Is it true or false that the government is now refusing to buy the electricity they generate? With demand currently high, purchasing more wind power from private producers would substantially reduce power cuts. Is it true or false that, instead of buying cheaper wind power, the AIADMK government is trying to purchase electricity from other states at a higher price?
With affection,
M. K.
27 October 2013
